en.title: Info

quotes.text: I am not what happened to me, I am what I choose to become.
quotes.author: Carl Gustav Jung

quotes.text: No problem can be solved from the same level of consciousness that created it.
quotes.author: Albert Einstein

quotes.text: Where consciousness (attention) wanders, there energy flows.
quotes.author: Gurudeva

en.why:
We possess a transcendental component of consciousness in the form of the 'self',and thus become partially conscious beings—though approximately 95% of mental processes occur beyond conscious control, on the level of the unconscious. We are territorial, social, and logical-emotional beings, endowed with the ability to create on three planes of reality: the physical (objective, material, measurable), the inner (subjective, immaterial, immeasurable), and the social (intersubjective, constituted by agreement, not by fact). To this end, we possess three forms of cognition: cleverness (practical orientation in the world), reason (analytical thinking), and wisdom (transcending particulars toward the whole).

The mind shapes the psyche, which is grounded in emotions—these do not arise in real time through conscious choice, but simply happen to us. We experience who and where we are, what we create, and how we perceive both ourselves and the world around us—and this experience is reflected back into the world and into ourselves. It is a constant process of becoming. If we do not identify with the contents of consciousness or with external circumstances, we remain ourselves—in our most authentic form—and reality can naturally work in our favor. The only thing we are always capable of is taking the next step consciously: thought → word → deed → habit → character → destiny. 

Deep within us lie the answers to fundamental questions: why we are here and what meaning our lives hold. Without being rooted in meaning, direction, conviction, and faith, nothing can endure. What is the inexhaustible source of strength and courage in your life—independent of anything external?

ℹ️
en.noteWhy: E.g. joy from helping others and spreading light — goodness, truth, self.
en.body:
Only on this foundation does the question of “how” arise—and this has nothing to do with technique, but rather with a way of life. Discipline is a form of love for what we want to become. We cannot overlook the order of reality, for it is precisely with this order that every “how” works. “How” is not an impersonal matter: it is an expression of who we are. Every action—good or bad—shapes us, and should not sever our roots. Because “how” accompanies us throughout our lives, there are methods and a variety of steps in reality—what. Simply put: we experience either a state (body, fact, matter) or a situation (relationship, inclination, time)—and if we realize this, they can always be placed within the context of reality: integrated through an intention that is in harmony with who we are and why we are here—fusion.

The 'self' is such a natural part of our existence that we easily overlook it. The self can be understood as a reflection of the world within us—not as the content of the image of the world itself. How many times have we acted without this aspect being fully present with us? If we enter a state of flow during an activity—that is, a pure experience of skill with a clear goal and immediate feedback—we cannot speak of overlooking the self: we are both the reflection and the content. When something threatens us, the “I” component helps us and protects the boundaries of our integrity. However, as we know, the mind’s defense mechanisms can lead to an unhealthy separation from the energy of the natural flow of life.

featuredQuote.text: Know thyself and you will know the world (you are that world).
featuredQuote.author: Oracle at Delphi
